title: Game Theory
definition: The study of mathematical models of strategic interaction between rational decision-makers. It analyzes situations where an individual's success depends on the choices of others, identifying optimal strategies and equilibrium outcomes.
formula: Nash equilibrium: ∀i, u_i(s_i*, s_-i*) ≥ u_i(s_i, s_-i*)

proofs.description: Nash's proof that every finite game has at least one Nash equilibrium
proofs.reference: Nash, J.F. (1950). Equilibrium Points in N-Person Games. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 36(1), 48-49.
von Neumann, J. (1928). Zur Theorie der Gesellschaftsspiele. Mathematische Annalen, 100, 295-320.

difficulty_levels.beginner: Game theory studies how smart people make decisions when their choices affect each other. It helps predict what will happen in situations like negotiations or competitions.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: Game theory provides formal tools for analyzing strategic interactions. Key concepts include Nash equilibrium (stable strategy profiles), dominant strategies, and Pareto optimality.
difficulty_levels.expert: Game theory extends from rational choice models to evolutionary dynamics, behavioral deviations, and mechanism design. Applications include auction design, voting systems, network formation, and AI multi-agent systems.

extra_fields.historical_context: Originated with von Neumann's minimax theorem (1928). Major contributions from Nash (1950), Shapley, and Harsanyi. John Nash's work earned the Nobel Prize (1994).
extra_fields.limitations: Assumes rational, self-interested agents with common knowledge. Behavioral economics shows humans deviate from pure rationality.
